A Monday puzzle that didn't try to do too much, and as a result, was a pretty good Monday puzzle.

63A!

Hey!

I got one.
Been about 25 years since I last used DOPE as slang for cool, surprised it immediately popped up in my mind.  This  felt like a run of the mill Monday puzzle.

An unreturned serve is not necessarily an ace. An ace is a serve that the receiver is unable to touch at all with their racket. Unreturned means unable to hit the ball in play. A serve that is hit, but not put into play by the receiver, is not an ace.

Perfect for a Monday. Love the theme, and especially love how all the themers start with words for small but none of them are stand-alone in the word or phrase—that makes it especially interesting and clever, and I thought the themers were interesting entries and clues in their own right. 
Two sports related nits. Andrew is right, below, an unreturned served is often an ACE but frequently not, so just changing that clue to “Unreturned serve, often” or something like that would fix it. By the same token, a quarterback tackle is only a SACK if it happens behind the line of scrimmage. If the quarterback runs out with the ball on a running play and then gets tackled, it’s not a sack. So, “tackle of the quarterback, usually” would fix that. Not a big deal, both answers are pretty obvious, but why not be more accurate if you can be?

For those on iPhones who find that after completing the Crossword, Midi or Mini that they are stuck on the Congratulations screen with an inoperable “X” in upper right corner of screen this might help your situation:
As far as I can determine this has to do with the iPhone Display Zoom setting being set to “Larger Text”. 
If you go to your Settings app, then “Display & Brightness”, scroll down to “Display Zoom” and toggle it to “Default”. Then click the Blue Check. 
You then get a pop-up that says ‘Changing Display Zoom will Restart iPhone’, click ‘Standard’. Screen will go dark for a few seconds as display resets (it doesn’t ‘restart’ your phone btw). 
You now will be able to dismiss the crossword completion screen by clicking the X in upper right corner, as the game was designed to do.
Only downside is you lose the ‘Larger Text’ effect on the iPhone until you toggle it back. 


I tried creating a Shortcuts automation that would be able to do the aforementioned falderal with a click or two but no luck. 
Alas ….

(Hoping the next NYTimes Games app has a fix 🤞)
Fun easy puzzle.  I thought I might have a shot at a Monday PR but had “Something’s amiss” on MOUE.  No idea what that is.
What a perfect and engaging Monday grid! I had a bit of a tough time with “Weed Whacker,” though, but found the theme to be lovely and easily identifiable. Great job!
Thank you for the new PB! Got a 2:58

Fun!  First time I didn't look anything up. #StartSmall
Little and cute!
Nice one, Rena! Short and sweet, with a couple of minor challenges to make it interesting.  Thanks!
With apologies for my ignorance about all things American and all things related to (American) football, why is SACK the answer to “Tackling of the quarterback”?
Great Monday puzzle, thank you!
@G, Tracy Ullman was on Elspeth for the second time (two different characters) tonight!  😃

Cute theme.
And there was a Roo.
My kind of Monday.

I believe Steve L defined "Too Close Syndrome" as complaining about a crossword clue/answer because you're too well versed in the topic and see the answer as invalid from a technical perspective, as opposed to a lay understanding of the topic.

I'd like to define a concept called overeducated=overthinking. It's when you're too well-versed in a topic that you miss the most obvious answer since you're looking for a more niche response.

That was me with 40A

My first thought was Wizengamot, but I couldn't remember now to spell it and was too short. My second thought was magicalcongress, which is the American equivalent, heavily featured in the newer Fantastic Beast movies. It just so happened to be the right number of letters. The one most heavily featured in the books, movies, and theme parks didn't even odor occur to me until I had a bunch of crossings.

commentBody: Ms. Cohen seems to bring a touch of elegance to puzzles even on the simplest of days.

I had a streak of 44 gold stars through this past week, but alas…reality reared its unwelcome head on a devious Thursday and continued to remind me of my limits through Sunday.

“Poor Icarus!—to soar so high,
Then fall! For you 't was vain to try…” -Florence Earle Coates
Easy Monday, except for MOUE, but I didn’t MOPE, I just OUSTed the offending P.

This puzzle made me philosophical, remembering the Tao Te Ching's words about starting small:

"Lay plans for the accomplishment of the difficult before it becomes difficult. Make something big by starting with it when small. Difficult things in the world ... have their beginning in the easy; big things have their beginnings in the small."
Lao Tzu, Tao Te Ching, Chapter 63; D. C. Lau translation.

By the month of May, many people who had New Year's resolutions have already abandoned course. 
Usually it's because of too much too soon, too many things all at once, and too drastic a change. If only they had started small...

Every day is the beginning of a new year. Every day, a new chance to START SMALL. 

Thank you for the reminder, Ms. Cohen.
And for a great Monday puzzle as well!
Monday PB!! 4:49. Approaching competent 😁

Whizzed through this, so unlike yesterday’s tussle, but then, it is Monday.
A delightful puzzle, nothing WEE about the theme, which was simple but elegant. 
Re: CHARD, hmm, it’s not sold as a salad veg here, definitely needs cooking à la pak choi, older spinach etc. I wonder if we use the same name for a different type of leafy veg? The wonders of two countries split by a common language continues.

Any grid that can hold a ROO, a koala and Harry Potter gets my vote. Throw in an Alpaca and I’m a happy bunny. Unlike my Alpacas, who not only have had the trauma of TB testing (still awaiting results) but suffered the indignity of shearing on Saturday. The poor dears look like malformed camels now; all leg and no hump.
Here we go dissing cats again. Cats are very social creatures by nature; most of them are unpleasant only to people who don't like cats.

One can tell Rena Cohen is an expert at these things--here we have a Monday-level puzzle with remarkably little GLUE--sure, we have a couple of unusually names celebrities (AVA, ISSA), a texting abbreviation (CYA), and half a brand name (KAT), but all the rest are perfectly good, *real* words that get used in everyday conversation--well, at least among pouty UCLA professors of Latin and biology, that is.
And although I picked up on 'BABY" and "TOY," I missed "MINI" and "WEE" until I filled in the revealer.
Would like to have seen 20A clued as [____ is____is ____].
Nice puzzle to start the week with!  Could there be a typo in the clueing of 64D ?  (On line)

Hi all!
Great to be back with an easy Monday puzzle!
Hope all of you are happy and well!

xoxoxo
B
Two Monday gold stars. Hoorah!

Rena’s puzzle has elements that lift it above Monday-ordinary.
•	Originality. While it’s a common theme type (“What do the starts of the theme answers have in common”), it’s a new iteration, I believe.  Terrific idea!
•	Pop.  Three of the theme answers are NYT debuts and the fourth (BABYLONIAN) is a once-before.
•	Elegance.  The theme is very tight.  There aren’t many synonyms for small, and far fewer that can be hidden at the beginning of words.
•	Skill. Those “small” synonyms are very well hidden in the words that encase them.
•	Entertainment. There are two theme riddles that can be cracked, to great satisfaction.  First, spotting the “small” beginnings, and second, guessing the revealer, which often is very hard to do, but today it’s within reach.
•	Junk-be-gone.  The only glue in the grid is GLUE (Hi, @Bill!).

Your puzzle, Rena, as all your grids, had a sweet feel to it, but the added notes of competence and talent made it high quality as well.  Thank you for a splendid outing!

I wonder if there has ever been a weekday puzzle with more (non-thematic) entries starting with A?

AGAR, AVIS, AROSE, AREA, AXE, AIOLI, ALOE, ASTRO, AMIGO, ANNUAL, AVA, ADIEU, ACE

Probably, but that's quite ALLOT.
I love Swiss chard but have never eaten it in a salad. 🤷🏻‍♀️

Question for fellow NYT Crossword people: today I noticed that the clues listed to the right of the puzzle didn’t match the clues shown above the grid for 16-Across and 54-Across. Was that intentional? Some kind of theme/trick I’m missing, or just a glitch? It threw me off for a minute. 🤔

After I replaced IN OT at 1-Across with TIED, I filled in the NE pretty quickly -- not unusual for a Monday -- and tackled the first themer with BABY- in the grid.  I spent a few seconds wondering how the "Code of Hammurabi" could have anything to do with BABies, before the penny dropped.

That second, however, gave me a good head-start on understanding the theme before I got to the revealer, and helped me put in MINI-STRY, since I'm not a Harry Potter  fan.

Even so, when I put in WEED WHACKER, I paused for a moment, thinking that this one didn't fit in with the others.
Oh, I thought, now I see it!

This is my way of saying that the theme, although Monday simple and straightforward, did a good job of hiding its SMALL parts well.

Thanks for a good start to the week, Rena Cohen.

Feels like I’ve been doing these forever but my best time for Monday is still 11:42. BABY steps led to WEE better times but to get a 5 minute solve I need to head to the MINI. 

Have a great solving week y’all! 😉

My excuse for this is:  I only had one sip of coffee.

I sat here trying to figure out how WEED fit in with the other terms for SMALL.

Plus I woke up at 4:45 a.m. and couldn't go back to sleep after Wordle and Spelling Bee because of the CAT, who saw the sky growing brighter in the EAST.  (How come MY kitty isn't antisocial, aloof, independent, and self-sufficient?....)

Happy Monday, everyone!

Remembering with embarrassment when I, arms flapping erratically, galumphed into the Ministry of Magic, having meant to go next door to the Ministry of Silly Walks.

Took me a spell to realize my mistake.

Fun Monday puzzle. Fairly smooth solve with working the crosses, and... the reveal was one of the last things I filled in and when I got it I had to go back and review the other theme answers until it finally dawned on me. That's always a nice touch. 

And I'll put my puzzle find today in a reply. 

...

Here in SW VA they call them 'weedeaters'. This has given rise to one of those horrible back-formed verbs: 'to weedeat'.
Usually this irks me (you are tending bar! Not 'bartending'! Ugh!).
But I wouldn't want my neighbor to request that I 'eat weeds'. Doesn't seem nice.

WEEDWHACKER obviates that issue.

Yesterday's constructor, Derrick Neiderman, posted in yesterday's comment, which some may be interested in reading. It's about 10 or 12 posts down presently, reading from newest to oldest.

I'm afraid I have a slight beef with 12-down.
Au revoir means, literally, "Until we see each other again." It implies confidence that you will meet the person again in the near future.
Adieu comes from the Old French "à Dieu vous commant," meaning "I entrust you to God." It is used to say farewell to someone; i.e., for what you know will be a long time, and possibly forever.

A week on from giving up my long streaks and badge-hunting, it's really nice and recommendable. Just finished the printed version of yesterday's crossword - that was an enjoyable monster!
If there's anything in your life nagging at you, no matter how small, cut it out!

I liked the theme but overall was not super impressed. Like some noted, some of the answers were stretches. 

Meh…

Rating: 
6/10
I'm always extremely disappointed when crosswords reference Harry Potter. It's not okay. I don't care if it was your childhood, move on to something that doesn't signal support for bigotry.
To make it a bit tougher, didn't look at the down clues.  Took about 15 minutes . . .

One more puzzle. Can't imagine having much a chance at this one. A Sunday from December 25, 1994 by Randolph Ross with the title "Joy to the world." The theme answers in this one were all "Season's greetings" in different languages. Some answers: KALACHRISTOUGENNA MELEKALIKIMAKA CHOOKSUNGTAN JOYEUXNOEL BUONNATALE GLEDELIGJUL NOLLAIGSHONADHUIT and MERRYCHRISTMAS Whew. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=12/25/1994&g=100&d=A ....

Not about crosswords, but I haven't found where to  comment on "tiles."  I would prefer to see the number of errors made in solving, rather than (or in addition to) length of longest streak of correct guesses.  It would give us finer grained feedback on how we did.
Short snd sweet.  MOUE threw me.  That's a new one for me.  My mind went to MOPE.

Today's poem made from words found in today’s puzzle

a/ there’s a cockroach in the city
in the city of the universe
calm the cockroach
unambitious
and the cake of sunrise joined the west
d/ in a sympathy of instruments
ta-Ta! ta-Ta!
these are my history moments
these are my famous years
throw out the long chapters
a/ adieu adieu

Very clever puzzle. I enjoyed it.  Never had an unsociable cat, however. I f they weren’t nudging equally sociable cats off my lap, or jumping onto a seated visitor, they were poking their noses into whatever the plumber was doing under the kitchen sink, climbing up the step ladder behind the painter, etc.
Real easy, or as a solver on yesterday's crossword might say, BABYISH.
My fastest Monday! I always sit down and play "Gonna Fly Now" and try to get my best time. Flew through this one, thankfully knew all the downs to get MOUE.
Transphobia in the comments is disappointing but not surprising. Y’all need to do better.

As usual on Monday, I tried to do this puzzle using only the Downs. I was quickly thwarted, and I wonder if the constructor deliberately made the down clues hard. Generally, a great Monday puzzle with a nice theme and a broad spectrum of clues to please all solvers.
MOUE?? For a Monday puzzle? GTFOOH.

My favorite encore clues from last week:

[Races against the clock]  (4)(6)
[Give a hand?]  (5)


TIME TRIALS
SHAKE

@Lewis. Interesting to see that in French time trials are “contre la montre” literally “against the clock”. 
And yes I am looking forward to Le Tour.

@Lewis Ohhh! I don't think I understood [Trigger hair] = MANE until seeing it again just now. In my defense, I've been traveling for the past week and trying to keep up with the puzzle, so less ruminating and lovely lingering than I am usually afforded. I've got my saddle on my horse He's called (T-t-t-t-t-trigger!) of course. https://youtu.be/WtbqbQawo-A?si=bL_QJ1UULuyO6OST (Yeah, I'm not a Gen-Xer for nuthin!) Great list, and thanks for that!!

@Steve L

Well, it had a pretty neat theme.
It did that!

Please emu.  Allow me to submit this!

B

@Bonnie

Hey there!  I haven’t seen you in a while. Hope everything is ok at the Jersey Shore.

Francis,
I just knew you could do it.

@Francis
Me too!

@Jane Wheelaghan

High five!  ✋

Daniel,
Interesting. You just described having an experience with this puzzle that was hardly run of the mill.

Andrew,
The clue did not specify a racket sport.
N.B. In volleyball, a serve that is "hit but not put into play by the receiver" *is* considered an ace.
@Barry Ancona Okay, I didn't know that.  It's possible, though unlikely, the author was talking about volleyball.
@Heidi Agree to disagree

@SP

Had the clue just been “a serve”, I would agree that “ace” would be a bit of a stretch. But “unreturned serve” narrows the field, and that’s why I think it works. Much like in your example of “black and white animal” vs just “animal”. 

I also agree that there are other answers that could fit the definition, but isn’t figuring out the right fit (based on crosses) a common task in completing a crossword? 

As I understand the original objection, the position was that “ace” could not be considered to be an Unreturned Serve. But since an ace is never returned, I don’t see why not. Am I missing something?

Andrew,
I have no idea what the constructor (or editors) had in mind; I only read the clue.

Heidi,
The original objection was that (in tennis, but not volleyball) not all unreturned serves are aces. Nobody is suggesting an ace is not an unreturned serve.

Is this doubles?
@Heidi In the days of old, any given name could be clued [Girl’s name] or [Boy’s name]. For example: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=7/31/1966&g=81&d=A Thankfully, they don’t do that anymore.

@Steve L

Wow that’s fascinating, looking at that puzzle. I’m for sure not going to be doing any 1966 puzzles any time soon, it’s like a whole different type of experience (and one I wouldn’t like). It gives me perspective about how new solvers might feel about hard puzzles from ten years ago.

@Andrew

Your comment is correct but if it's a "not necessarily true" situation then there is no problem. You will get an argument to the death if you claim otherwise.
@Andrew I’ll just add, as a rabid volleyball fan: harrumph.

SP,
Did you miss my response to Andrew about the ACE?

@Barry Ancona

I saw your first response but not the second—great catch, I couldn’t think of another sport where you had an ace without a racket. My nit about SACK still stands though.

SP,
Does "It's a clue, not a definition" ring a bell? Do you really have a "nit" about the SACK clue, or are you just making a Fact Boy comment about it?

@Elbridge Gerry

It’s just Barry, I’m cool.
I started a treatise below about how some clues seem to be more naturally geared to definitions and some to examples, but no need to belabor it. 
Yes, it’s more of a fact boy comment and an FYI. Like I said it would have been easy to fix and not a big deal. I just don’t want Andrjez watching football when we do an international game in Poland and complaining that the defense wasn’t awarded a sack after they tackled the quarterback in a QB sneak. 
Barry to me a nit is just a clue that bugged me slightly and that’s it. When I think a clue is totally wrong you will know it.
This is what that is: https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/moue

@Chris

Just watch any Audrey Hepburn movie…

@Chris

You should be able to look up moue and see the funny pursed lips!

hehehe
B

commentBody:

Or Meryl Streep's character in Devil Wears Prada 1 or 2, who provides moues aplenty.

May this be the first of many no-lookups!👏
About half of Mondays I can now do without lookups. It took a long time for me to get to this plateau, but the view is pretty nice.😊 
11:24 time on my phone.
No doubt would be faster with a keyboard — fewer if any typos.

@Petrol because that’s the word for tackling a QB. possibly  from to sack someone is to fire them. Both slang. 
L

@Petrol 
Had to look this up myself, because it is well known to us. Reportedly started in the 1960s by a prominent defensive player and is related to the devastation caused by sacking a city.
@Paul Thanks! I shall be on the lookout for a Thursday clue “Apt name for a quarterback”, the answer being TROY
@LarryF I have doubled my knowledge of (American) football today, thank you!

@Petrol

Actually, a SACK is only when a QB is tackled behind the line of scrimmage; that is, for a loss of yardage on the play.  The QB can run with the ball and gain yards, in which case he is usually eventually tackled, and that's not considered a SACK.

@Petrol
Actually, there was a quarterback named Troy Aikman. He played for the Dallas Cowboys.  And back in the 2000s, his last name was in a clue and Troy was in the puzzle.

@Steve L

TIL!

@Francis

So much for Minnesota nice.

@Petrol
Here is my (hard-earned) knowledge:

When the QB (quarterback) is hit by a member of the opposing team, preventing his passing the football for a "Touchdown" --or a mere "Down"-- he is said to be SACKed.  Of course, his GUARDS are supposed to protect him from such assault, but speed and agility in the other team can overcome "The Defense."  

PhysicsDaughter once told me (witheringly)--"Mom, if you just learn the basics, I am sure you would enjoy football."  Hah.

@Francis

Anyway, you guys used to have a pretty good QB named Francis on your state's team...

@Petrol,

“The sacking of Troy” sounds like something a sports announcer would have come up with in Aikman’s playing days!

@HeathieJ

Whuuuut?!?? And I missed them both?!?? (Or all three, if you're counting characters?) Gah, I love when she plays multiple characters!! 

I guess it's time to get Paramount+, so I can watch reruns. 

(Normally on Sunday nights I'm watching CBS. But I'm out of town and off my schedule.)

Thanks for the heads-up!! 😘

commentBody:
@sotto voce

"Every day is the beginning of a new year." 

What a lovely sentiment! I am up with the sun today, unusual for me, and there was a cacophony of birdsong accompanying me when I arose. So industrious!

They, and you (or at least your words), and all the Spring blooming around me certainly make the day feel like the cusp of something brand new. Thanks for that!

@sotto voce

Any comment that references the Old Master is all right in my little book!

@sotto voce

Nice thought. Here are some other versions of the same idea.

A stitch in time saves nine.
A journey of a thousand miles starts with a single step.
Don't put the cart before the horse.
Don't lock the barn door after the horse has gone away.
Gather ye rosebuds while ye may.
Perseverance furthers.

Maybe?

@G
...and she expresses herself in just as lovely a way in person!

@G

How synchronous! 
I hope the brand new thats starting is ripe with the joyful and fulfilling.

That you've had this wonderful feeling is in and of itself significant. All small things add up. 😉

@Mean Old Lady

Aw, thank you! 💕
@Rory Got myself a new record today as well, 5:54! Very smooth Monday puzzle.

@Bill
Interesting. I have grown Chard but find it too fibrous to eat raw. Perhaps it comes down to personal taste then, though I can’t say I’ve ever seen it in a salad when eating out.

@Helen Wright

That clue was flat-out wrong, in my opinion. But Bill is right: the baby chard in "spring mix" boxes of  salad are the best bits! No more than an inch in length plus an extension of red stem, they are ovoid and almost plush, tender and so flavorful. But the adult version with the adult name Swiss chard is exactly as you describe. If I had better knife skills and could chiffonade, perhaps I'd try Fred's salad; meanwhile, that stuff is nowhere near being eaten raw in my house!
@Helen Wright across the pond, folks call it bok choy.
@Charles, I believe that is a different vegetable,

@G
Research has shown that a BABY comes with an in-born temperament (see Chess, Thomas, and Birch's NY Longitudinal Study)....so why not our cats?  Our experience is that intelligence is also variable, LOL.

@Katie
I counter with this tidbit about my cat, Tom Mix:  I was stung on the forehead by a bee, resulting in my entire face's being badly swollen, with mere slits for eyes.  Tom Mix looked at me very closely, and then reached out and very gently patted my puffy cheek with a gentle paw.
He also stayed by me during the Benadryl-induced naps.

Like humans, cats respond to socialization in early life.
@NanuNanu …and extremely friendly to the allergic!

commentBody:
@NanuNanu

Apparently if you're a quadruped and you're not fawning or servile then you are antisocial.

@Bill

CYA means something completely different to me.

@Steve L

California Youth Association?

@Steve L
LOL  I did a double-take for that one!

@The X-Phile

As you can see, I'm from New York, so no...

@Steve L,
Agreed. CYA will always have a special place amongst TLAs after 35+ years in corporate America.
@Edwin (didn’t see a typo on the app version…)

@Edwin Bounding means running with big, powerful strides. So a kangaroos bounds when it runs. (Yes, it also bounces! Or hops.)

Years ago I asked my daughter if she wanted to go see a kangaroo exhibition at the Virginia Museum of Fine Arts. Confused, she replied, "kangaroos?!" I replied yes, the museum banner says "Exhibition of Hoppers."
@Michael G. Many thanks!  When I looked up Bounder, I found a morally reprehensible person.  So I assumed bouncer were intended.

@Edwin

If you think of the phrase "progressing by leaps and 'bounds'", you'll see the connection.
Amusingly (?), progress by leaps and bounds is a pretty good antonym for START SMALL.
"Leaps and bounds" is a nice example of the figure of speech called "hendiadys" (literally "one-through-two"), one of my favorite words.

@Steve L
"Bounder" (or "cad" or "rotter") in the UK means a wrong 'un  - Victorian upper-class slang - someone lacking manners, morals and good breeding.

@Bonnie

Welcome back!

@Bonnie

What @sotto voce said! 

Nice to "see" you again. It's been a while. 
:)

@L
It's not as tasty as arugula or the leaf lettuces, but in a pinch it works all right.  I prefer fresh (raw) greens to those that are cooked.  I pick CHARD when it's young.

@Jeanie K

They appear to match for me ... in the online puzzle viewed with Chrome.

Now that I believe that you're not as "mean" as you pretend to be, I'm happy to call you KIN.

@ad absurdum having dabbled in stage magic and consequently been around magicians' institutions, silly extravagant gestures will make you fit in just fine. After hours spent rehearsing illusion choreography it's hard to turn it off.

I think you are mixing up your Monty Python sketches. And if you don’t agree with me, arguments is down the hall

@Rich in Atlanta As threatened - a Thursday from August 26, 2021 by Ashish Vengsarkar. Don't recall another one quite like this. Some examples: Down answer-- MAL(I) crossing SUR(I)NAME both of those clued as MAL and SURNAME and.. NO(R)WAY crossing (R)WANDA And both of those clued as NOWAY and WANDA. One other example: TONG(A) crossing PANAM(A) Both of those clued of course as TONG and PANAM And then the reveal: "One way to run... or a hint to four geographical intersections in this grid." commentBody:
@Michael
So, if you're not ill, I shouldn't greet you with 'Salut!'?

@Stephen
Piffle. I enjoyed Harry Potter, and I agree with JK Rowling. George Bernard Shaw was a eugenicist, who advocated for the eradication of the "unfit" in the advancement of Socialism. That's a far cry from saying boys shouldn't play in girls' sports.

@SP While I agree with you that there's no need to exclude Harry Potter from crossword puzzles because of the author's opinions or behavior, I'm not sure a comparison with Shakespeare, Twain, or slave-owning presidents is the best argument, because unlike all of them, Rowling is alive and still profits from Harry Potter. (Not that I think she profits from Harry Potter being an entry in a crossword, but still.)

@Bob

Exactly. You don’t want to buy or read Harry Potter, that’s entirely your prerogative. But I don’t think JR Rowling is going to make a dime more in royalties because MINISTRYOFMAGIC is mentioned in a crossword, and if we start policing our puzzles for authors or other celebrities we disagree with, we are all doomed.
